note_data.title: Logarithms
keyPoints: Logarithm is the inverse operation of exponentiation — if aˣ = N, then log_a N = x
Common logarithm has base 10 (log N) and natural logarithm has base e (ln N)
Product rule: log_a (MN) = log_a M + log_a N
Quotient rule: log_a (M/N) = log_a M - log_a N
Power rule: log_a (Mⁿ) = n log_a M
Change of base formula: log_a N = (log_b N)/(log_b a)
log_a 1 = 0 and log_a a = 1 for any base a > 0, a ≠ 1
Antilogarithm is the inverse of logarithm — if log N = x, then antilog x = N
Logarithmic equations can be solved by converting to exponential form

definitions.definition: The logarithm of a positive number N to a positive base a (a ≠ 1) is the exponent to which the base a must be raised to get the number N. If aˣ = N, then log_a N = x.
A logarithm with base 10 is called common logarithm. It is written as log N instead of log₁₀ N.
A logarithm with base e (where e = 2.718...) is called natural logarithm. It is written as ln N or log_e N.
If log N = x, then N is called the antilogarithm of x. We write N = antilog x or N = 10ˣ.

importantQuestions.question: Define logarithm and write its properties.
importantQuestions.answer: Logarithm of a positive number N to base a is the exponent x such that aˣ = N. Properties: log_a(MN) = log_a M + log_a N, log_a(M/N) = log_a M - log_a N, log_a(Mⁿ) = n log_a M

importantQuestions.question: Solve the equation log₂(x+3) + log₂(x-1) = 3 and verify your answer.
importantQuestions.answer:
log₂(x+3) + log₂(x-1) = 3
Using product rule: log₂[(x+3)(x-1)] = 3
Converting to exponential form: (x+3)(x-1) = 2³ = 8
x² + 2x - 3 = 8
x² + 2x - 11 = 0
Using quadratic formula: x = (-2 ± √48)/2 = -1 ± 2√3
Since x > 1 (for log to be defined), x = -1 + 2√3
Verification: Substitute back in original equation to confirm.

commonMistakes: Students confuse log_a b with log_b a — remember the base is written as subscript
Forgetting domain restrictions — logarithm is only defined for positive numbers
Mixing up product and quotient rules — log(MN) = log M + log N, not log M × log N
Not converting to exponential form when solving logarithmic equations
Calculating antilog incorrectly — if log N = 2, then N = 10², not 2 × 10
examTips: This chapter usually has 1-2 MCQs and 1 short question worth 3-4 marks in BSEK papers
Always write the logarithmic properties before applying them in solutions
Practice converting between logarithmic and exponential forms — very common in exams
Check your answers by substituting back, especially in logarithmic equations
Memorize the values: log₂ 8 = 3, log₃ 27 = 3, log₁₀ 100 = 2, log₁₀ 1000 = 3
note_data.keyDiagram: Graph of y = log_a x showing: domain (0, ∞), range (-∞, ∞), passes through (1, 0) and (a, 1), increasing function when a > 1, decreasing when 0 < a < 1
